title: SEGMENT 16 — Resistance — antigen loss and efflux

lines.text: SEGMENT 16 — Resistance — antigen loss and efflux
ADCs, like every other class of cancer drug, eventually fail.
Some patients respond initially and then progress.
Some patients never respond at all.
Understanding how cancer cells become resistant to ADCs is an active area of research, and several mechanisms have been characterized.
The simplest mechanism is antigen loss.
The cancer cells stop expressing the target protein.
If the antigen isn't there, the antibody has nothing to bind, no internalization happens, and no payload is delivered.
This is especially common with heterogeneous tumors where the original tumor had a mix of antigen-high and antigen-low cells.
The antigen-high cells get killed by the ADC, but the antigen-low cells survive and eventually become the dominant population.
Selection in action.
For T-DM1, antigen loss is a major mechanism of resistance, because the drug has no bystander effect — only the antigen-high cells get killed in the first place.
For T-DXd, the bystander effect mitigates this somewhat, because even antigen-low cells can be killed through diffusion of released payload.
But over time, even tumors treated with bystander-capable ADCs can lose enough antigen expression to become resistant.
A second mechanism is drug efflux.

Cancer cells often upregulate transporters — particularly the ATP-binding cassette transporters like P-glycoprotein (also called MDR1 or ABCB1), MRP1, and BCRP — that actively pump small molecules out of the cell.
Once the payload is released inside a target cell, an efflux pump can grab it and pump it back out before it does damage.
This is a classic chemotherapy resistance mechanism, and it applies to ADC payloads too, because the payloads are typically substrates for the same transporters that handle conventional chemotherapy.
Different payloads have different susceptibility to efflux.
MMAE, the auristatin in Adcetris, is a known P-glycoprotein substrate, and P-gp upregulation is a known resistance mechanism.
DM1 is also a P-gp substrate.
The DXd payload appears to be a weaker P-gp substrate, which may contribute to T-DXd's broader efficacy.
Choosing payloads that are poor efflux substrates is one of the design considerations going into newer ADCs.
A third mechanism is defective trafficking.
The cancer cell may internalize the ADC normally but then fail to deliver it to the lysosome efficiently.
Endocytic vesicles may get recycled back to the cell surface without ever fusing with the lysosome.
The ADC gets re-secreted instead of digested.
No payload is released.
This is harder to detect clinically but has been documented in resistant cell lines.
A fourth mechanism is payload-specific resistance.
Tumors may upregulate the molecular target of the payload — for example, downregulating topoisomerase I to reduce sensitivity to DXd, or mutating tubulin to reduce sensitivity to MMAE.
These are slow-developing resistance mechanisms but they have been observed.
What do you do about resistance?
Several strategies are being pursued.
One is switching payload classes.
If a patient progresses on a tubulin-targeting ADC, switching to a topo I-targeting ADC with the same antibody may restore activity.
The antibody-target axis still works; only the payload-target axis has failed.
T-DM1-failure patients often respond to T-DXd, even though both drugs target HER2, because they have different payloads.
Another is combination therapy.
Combining an ADC with a checkpoint inhibitor, or with another ADC targeting a different antigen, or with a small-molecule kinase inhibitor, may overcome resistance through complementary mechanisms.
Many such combinations are in trials.
A third is rotating ADCs.

Some emerging strategies involve cycling between two or more ADCs against different targets, exploiting the diversity of antigen expression in a tumor to prevent selection of any single resistant population.
Resistance is the central long-term challenge in ADC therapy.
The drugs work — beautifully — in the short term.
The question for the field is how to extend the durability of response from months to years.
That requires understanding resistance mechanisms and designing therapy strategies that anticipate them.
